coredata.dc:title: A comparative fluid flow characterisation in a low frequency/high power sonoreactor and mechanical stirred vessel
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Sajjadi, Baharak
Raman, Abdul Aziz Abdul
Ibrahim, Shaliza
coredata.dc:description: This study aims at analysing the jet-like acoustic streaming generated under low-frequency and high-power ultrasound irradiation and comparing it with fluid streaming generated by traditional mechanical mixing. The main characteristics of fluid flow, which include radial, axial and tangential terms of velocity and their effects on fluid flow pattern, pressure distribution, axial mixing time and turbulence intensity were considered at different power inputs. Both 3D CFD simulation and Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) were used in this study. The CFD results indicated that the jet-like acoustic streaming reached the velocity magnitude of 145cm/s at 400W, which reduced the mixing time to 1.38s. However, the minimum mixing time of 3.18s corresponding to the impeller rotational speed of 800RPM was observed for mechanical stirring. A uniform axial flow pattern was generated under ultrasound irradiation whereas the tangential flow pattern was more prominent in the stirred vessel. Besides, the highest turbulence was observed in the vicinity of the ultrasound transducer and impeller with the values of 138% and 82% for the ultrasonicator and stirred vessel, respectively. The predicted fluid flow pattern under ultrasound irradiation was in a reasonable agreement with that obtained from PIV, with a reasonable accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Mechanically stirred vessel
Turbulence
Fluid streaming
Computational fluid dynamics (CFD)
Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Computational domains of (a) ultrasonicator modelling (b) stirred vessel modelling
Distribution of static pressure pulses within the system under ultrasound power of 100W.
Pressure distribution in stirred vessel with impeller velocity of 400RPM. (a) Static pressure, (b) dynamic pressure, (c) total pressure.
Vertical fluid flow pattern induced by ultrasound irradiation with power of 400W (a) presented by velocity vectors (b) presented by pathlines.
Vertical fluid flow pattern induced by stirred vessel with impeller velocity of 600RPM (a) presented by velocity vectors (b) presented by pathlines.
Horizontal fluid flow pattern induced by (left) stirred vessel with impeller speed of 600RPM (right). Induced by ultrasound irradiation with power of 400W presented by velocity vectors.

Distribution of axial velocity in different elevations from the bottom: (a) Ultrasound irradiation with power of 400W, (b) stirred vessel with impeller speed of 800RPM. (a) −: 7cm, ....: 6cm, ---: 5cm, - - -: 4cm, -.-.-.: 3cm, −−−: 2cm, −.−.−: 1 cm. (b) −: 9cm, ....: 7cm, ---: 6cm, - - -: 5cm, -.-.-.: 4cm, − − −: 3cm, −.−.−: 1cm.

Distribution of axial velocity in different elevations from the bottom. (a) Ultrasound irradiation with different power input, (b) stirred vessel with different impeller speed: (a) −: 6cm, 100W, ....: 5cm, 100W, ---: 6cm, 200W, - - -: 5cm, 200W, .-.-.: 6cm, 300W, − −: 5cm, 300W, .−.−: 6cm, 400W, ..−..−: 5cm, 400W. (b) −: 7cm, 400RPM, ....: 3cm, 400RPM, ---: 7cm, 600RPM - -: 3cm, 600RPM, .-.-.: 7cm, 800RPM, −−: 3cm, 800RPM

Distribution of radial velocity in different elevations from the bottom: a) Ultrasound irradiation with power of 400W, (b) Stirred vessel with impeller speed of 800RPM. (a) −: 7cm, ....: 6cm, ---: 5cm, - - -: 4cm, -.-.-.: 3cm, −−−: 2cm, −.−.−: 1cm. (b) −: 9cm, ....: 7cm, ---: 6cm, - - -: 5cm, -.-.-.: 4cm, −−−: 3cm, −.−.−: 1cm.

Distribution of radial velocity in different elevations from the bottom (a) Ultrasound irradiation with different power input, (b) stirred vessel with different impeller speed: (a) −: 6cm, 100W, ....: 5cm, 100W, ---: 6cm, 200W, - - -: 5cm, 200W, .-.-.: 6cm, 300W, −−: 5cm, 300W, .−.−: 6cm, 400W, ..−..−: 5cm, 400W; (b) −: 7cm, 400RPM, ....: 3cm, 400RPM, ---: 7cm, 600RPM - -: 3cm, 600RPM, .-.-.: 7cm, 800RPM, −−: 3cm, 800RPM.

Distribution of tangential velocity in Stirred vessel: (a) at different elevation with impeller speed of 800RPM, (b) under different impeller rotational speed. From the bottom: (a): − h =9cm, .... h =7cm, --- h =6cm, - - - h =5cm, -.-.-. h =4cm, −−− h =3cm, −.−.− h =1cm. (b): − h =3cm, 400RPM, .... h =7cm, 400RPM; --- h =3cm, 600RPM - - - h =7cm, 600RPM; -.-.-. h =3cm, 800RPM; −−− h =7cm, 800RPM.
The average velocity vectors field at 24kHz, input power (a) 100W, (b) 200W, (c) 300W, (d) 400W. A, B, C, D: PIV Analysing; a, b, c, d: CFD simulation.
Comparison between the velocity magnitude of acoustic jet-like streaming obtained through CFD simulation and PIV measurement at elevations of 6cm from the bottom. ♢: PIV, Δ: CFD.

Distribution of turbulence intensity in different elevations from the bottom (a) Ultrasonicator with different power input, (b) stirred vessel with different impeller speed: (a) −: 5cm, 400W, ....: 5cm, 300W, ---: 5cm, 200W, - - -: 5cm, 100W .-.-.: 3.5cm, 400W, −−: 3.5cm, 300W, .−.−: 3.5cm, 200W, ..−..−: 3.5cm, 100W. (b) −: 7cm, 400RPM, ....: 3cm, 400RPM, ---: 7cm, 600RPM - -: 3cm, 600RPM, .-.-.: 7cm, 800RPM, −−: 3cm, 800RPM.
Summary of investigated acoustic streaming under ultrasound irradiation in gas medium.

list-item.para: Displacement amplitude
Pressure waveforms
Pressure amplitude
Pressure distribution
Objective: Analysed the effect of non-linear standing wave
A multi-time-step, 6-point finite-numerical model was used
A second-order 1-D wave equation was considered
Conservation of the mass and momentum was considered
Displacement waveforms
Pressure waveforms
Amplitude distribution
Objective: Analysed the standing wave ranging from linear to strongly nonlinear and weak shock
The isentropic state equation of Tait-Kirkwood was used
Differential equation was solved without truncation
Mass and momentum conservation and absorption were considered
Harmonic distortion of pressure field effects
Nonlinear attenuation of pressure field effects
Objective: Evaluated the behaviour of strongly and complex nonlinear waves in thermoviscous fluid
Two-dimensional wave equation was considered
Redistribution effect of rms pressure inside a 2-D cavity was analysed
Pressure distribution inside the axisymmetric resonator
Pressure waveform at the centre of the reflector
Objective: Analysed strongly nonlinear standing acoustic waves in axisymmetric cavities at complicated modes of axisymmetric cavities
The wave equation was directly obtained from the conservation laws and state equation, by assuming an irrotational fluid
Pressure distribution in
(i) 2-Dresonator
(ii) Axisymmetric resonator
(iii) Planes of the 3-dresonator
Objective: Analysed nonlinear acoustic phenomena in high-power ultrasonic resonators
Possibility of modelling complicated nonlinear fields was illustrated in the one- two- and three-dimensional cases, including the axisymmetric configuration
Decomposition of the nonlinear pressure wave
Distribution of non-linear pressure wave
Displacement distribution
Objective: Calculated the linear to strongly non-linear (weak shock) acoustic field inside a 1-D resonant cavity
3 1-D numerical models in the time domain were employed

list-item.label: i.
list-item.para: Model 1. Implicit and conditionally stable model based on finite-difference schemes for both time and space derivatives

list-item.label: ii.
list-item.para: Model 2. Implicit and unconditionally stable model based on finite-difference scheme for time derivatives and a finite-volume scheme for approximating the spatial rates of change

list-item.label: iii.
list-item.para: Model 3. Based on the development of explicit techniques of high-order for the time integration
Acoustic radiated corresponds to resonance
far from resonance
Objective: Analysed the partial differential equations of 3-D linear and nonlinear acoustics in absorbing fluids
A formulation for coupling the Kuznetsov equation with the equations of motion of an elastic solid was also derived
Unsteady/steady shock waves
Nonlinear steepening of the waves
Damping effects
Objective: Analysed the cavitating flow caused by the oscillating wall
The 1-D model was based on coupling of conventional continuity and momentum equation into a Rayleigh–Plesset equation
Damping of the bubble volume oscillations was restricted to a simple ‘‘effective’’ viscosity. Damping of the bubble volume oscillations was restricted to a simple ‘‘effective’’ viscosity
Waveform distortion
Shock wave formation
Objective: Analysed the resonant oscillation of large amplitudes
The wave phenomena were characterised by the non-D geometrical parameter (Ri/Ro) and the acoustic Mach number
Resonant oscillation with/without damping
Objective: Analysed nonlinear shock free resonant oscillation of cylindrical and spherical standing waves
A cubic nonlinear equation for complex wave amplitude derived from the method of multiple scales
Acoustic pressure amplitude, variation of bubble volume
Objective: Analysed strongly nonlinear acoustic wave propagation in a bubbly liquid by coupling the nonlinear bubble vibration equation with the wave equation
Equations were coupled through the force terms. Then, nonlinear vibration of bubbles and pressure harmonic distortion were coupled and feeding-up

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Eu: Eulerian, L: Lagrangian, FVM: Finite Volume Method, FDM: Finite Difference Method, FEM: Finite Element Method.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of investigated acoustic streaming under ultrasound irradiation in liquid medium.

list-item.para: Variation of driving force of acoustic streaming
Nonlinearity parameter
Acoustic streaming velocity
Reynolds stresses
Radiation pressure
Objective: Analysed the dependency of acoustic streaming force on wave attenuation (linear/non-linear)
3D nonlinear wave propagation in a lossy, non-viscous fluid was presented for the analysis of acoustic streaming
The model was based on the wave equation of Kuznetsov
Force density and mean streaming velocity were obtained by time averaging of nonlinear pressure and velocity distributions
Nonlinear distortion of the finite amplitude wave was considered
Streaming velocity
Acoustic wave profile
Impact of wave attenuation
Reynolds stresses
radiation pressure
Objective: Analysed the linear/nonlinear wave propagation in high power ultrasound
Same as Ref [22]
Linear/non-linear pressure pulse and frequency spectrum
Impact of driving force on acoustic streaming
Objective: Analysed the acoustic streaming effects caused by Reynold’s stresses in the fluid medium
The driving model was based on Pierce and Kuznetsov’s equation

row.entry: Two circular rods, one rod subjected to axial monochromatic vibrations

list-item.para: Meniscus deformation
Vorticity generation in boundary layers near the rigid and free boundaries
Viscous damping of the surface waves
Spatial inhomogeneity of pulsation phase
Objective: Analysed the response of an isothermal liquid bridge to axial high-frequency vibrations of supporting rods
Model 1. Assuming that the vibrating liquid bridge had a cylindrical shape, a solution for the pulsating deformation of the free surface and the pulsating velocity field was found
Mean deformation of free surface due to vibrations was ignored
Resonant frequencies of free surface oscillation were calculated
Model 2. The mean deformation of the free surface was found by using a boundary-fitted curvilinear coordinate system
The non-linear equation was solved by using Newton’s method
Wave-shapes and Fourier decomposition of transient signals
Objective: Analysed the plane waves by using the full nonlinear equation without any restriction on the acoustic Mach number
Effects of absorption and loss were taken into account
The time domain model allowed simulations from linear to strongly nonlinear plane waves including weak shock

row.entry: Acoustic streaming induced by leaky surface waves

list-item.para: Wave fields
Wave amplitude in the fluid
Mean flows forced by the acoustic waves
Objective: Analysed the potential of streaming by leaky surface acoustic waves to generate mixing flows in micro device
A 2-D model of mean-flow generation by surface acoustic waves was driven
The model was based on the averaged vorticity equation (instead of momentum equation) to extract the balance between wave dissipation and mean-flow dissipation
The model was based on Eckart [33] and Westervelt [34] formulations
Mass fraction fields
Pressure distribution
Acoustic streaming velocity
Objective: Analysis of the Propagation of high-frequency (1.7MHz) ultrasound waves in a tubular sono-reactor
A 3-D CFD model was used to predict the flow pattern and micromixing efficiency in the plain tubular reactor
The acoustic pressure was modelled using Cai’s [10] formulation
Mass fraction fields
Volume fraction of microparticles
Acoustic streaming
Objective: Analysed the effect of microparticles on micromixing in a 1.7MHz tubular sonoreactor using CFD
The model was based on the kinetic theory of granular flow
Acoustic pressure was modelled using Cai et al.’s [10] formulation
Mixing time
Objective: Characterised micromixing quality under a 1.7MHz-transducer and did a comparison with the classic macro mixing
The acoustic pressure was modelled based on Cai’s [10] formulation
Cavitation volume fraction
Pressure amplitude
Pressure distribution
Objective: Analysed the influence of bubble distribution on the attenuation of the pressure field in a stagnant fluid
Objective: Analysed the impact of chamber configurations and process parameters on the sound intensity
The models were based on linearised/nonlinearised wave equation
The models were based on Wijngaarden [39] and Commander [40]’s model
Acoustic streaming velocity
Acoustic Pressure distribution
Objective: Characterised micromixing quality
The ultrasonic absorption coefficient was evaluated
The model was based on the time harmonic wave equation. (inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Defined boundary conditions in both systems

head.ce:title: A comparative fluid flow characterisation in a low frequency/high power sonoreactor and mechanical stirred vessel

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, University of Malaya, 50603 Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aims at analysing the jet-like acoustic streaming generated under low-frequency and high-power ultrasound irradiation and comparing it with fluid streaming generated by traditional mechanical mixing. The main characteristics of fluid flow, which include radial, axial and tangential terms of velocity and their effects on fluid flow pattern, pressure distribution, axial mixing time and turbulence intensity were considered at different power inputs. Both 3D CFD simulation and Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) were used in this study. The CFD results indicated that the jet-like acoustic streaming reached the velocity magnitude of 145cm/s at 400W, which reduced the mixing time to 1.38s. However, the minimum mixing time of 3.18s corresponding to the impeller rotational speed of 800RPM was observed for mechanical stirring. A uniform axial flow pattern was generated under ultrasound irradiation whereas the tangential flow pattern was more prominent in the stirred vessel. Besides, the highest turbulence was observed in the vicinity of the ultrasound transducer and impeller with the values of 138% and 82% for the ultrasonicator and stirred vessel, respectively. The predicted fluid flow pattern under ultrasound irradiation was in a reasonable agreement with that obtained from PIV, with a reasonable accuracy.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, high-power ultrasound irradiation has been introduced as an effective method for accelerating industrial processes that require mixing. Apart from generation of “micro-streaming”, “cavitation”, “shock wave” and “hot spot” [1], the sinusoidal pressure waves (the elements of ultrasound energy) may also cause a flow within the fluid, which is known as acoustic streaming. Acoustic streaming is inversely proportional to the speed of sound in the medium and the medium viscosity but it is proportional to the surface area of the ultrasound source, ultrasound power intensity and attenuation coefficient of the liquid bulk.

Eckart streaming, Gedeon streaming and Rayleigh streaming are types of acoustic streaming [2,3]. Eckart streaming (also known as “quartz wind”) originates from dissipation of acoustic energy in the fluid. It applies to large-scale phenomena and is generally found at frequencies above 1MHz. Gedeon streaming is normally observed in thermoacoustic travelling wave systems. In Gedeon streaming, a non-zero net mass transport occurs because of the phase difference between acoustic velocity and density. Rayleigh streaming, which is the focus of this study, is a sub-category of boundary layer driven acoustic streaming. It originates from the action of Reynolds stresses developing in the boundary layer near a solid wall in the fluid. Rayleigh streaming appears as a pair of symmetrical vortices extending along a quarter wave length. The scale of this type of streaming is characterised by the acoustic wavelength. The theoretical analysis of acoustic streaming was introduced by Rayleigh [4], and further developed by Riley [5] and Lighthill [6] who emphasised ways to dissipate the acoustic energy to the momentum flux gradient. [3,7] produced among the most complete reviews of different categories of acoustic streaming and their characteristics. Moreover, another critical review of nonlinear acoustics was presented by Beyer [8].

Propagation of acoustic waves in gas mediums and determination of their effects i.e. pressure balance or fluid flow within the medium have been extensively studied. A brief review of the numerical and computational works on acoustic streaming in gas and liquid mediums (from 2000 to 2015) is presented in Tables 1 and 2
, respectively. As observed, most of the numerical and analytical researches have been conducted in micro scales with one-dimensional tubes, rectangular-channels-enclosed tubes or chambers or open-scape gaps filled with an ideal gas.

This study presents a numerical and comparative study on propagation of acoustic streaming in the liquid medium inside a macro-scale sono-reactor. Two analyses were conducted: (i) numerical analysis of jet-like acoustic streaming using 3D CFD simulation; investigation of its effect on pressure balance within the system, axial, radial and tangential velocity compounds of liquid flow and mixing time; and (ii) practical analysis of jet-like acoustic streaming through Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV). The results were further compared with that of stirred vessel. In this study, the word “jet-like acoustic streaming” refers to high-speed fluid flow generated by ultrasound energy. This flow is initiated from the surface of the transducer toward the bottom of the vessel, exerting an impinging force on the liquid and pushing it to circulate in the vessel.

A Pyrex cylindrical-vessel (Diameter: 7.4cm and Height: 9.3cm) was used as a sono-reactor in the study. It was equipped with an ultrasonic horn, a condenser (to prevent evaporated liquid from going out from the reactor) and a thermometer (to control temperature). The sonication was performed with a 24kHz-ultrasonic processor UP400S (Hielscher Ultrasonics) with a power range of 100–400W. The vibrations were amplified by a sonotrode fitted to the horn, forming a
λ / 2
vibration that was transferred to the medium. Mechanical Stirring was also carried out in a similar Pyrex cylindrical-vessel with an overhead stirrer (13516 IKA Eurostar 60Digital) as a macro mixer. The stirred vessel was agitated by a pitched-blade-impeller with six 45°-blades with a blade width of W/D=0.2. The ratio of the impeller to the tank diameter was D/T=0.66 and the bottom clearance was C/T=0.65.

It should be noted that the focus of this study was only on investigating the ability of pure ultrasonication on mixing phenomena compared to pure mechanical mixing free of any geometrical effect such as baffle, angle of impeller/shaft/ultrasound probe or use of multi impeller/multi probe. Accordingly, the simplest and an identical geometry were selected for both systems. On the other hand, very high impeller motion and sonication power were considered in this study. In other words, the parameters related to geometrical effects, etc. were eliminated. Instead, the maximum ultrasound power and impeller speed that the system could support without any side effects (ie. vibration of the equipment) were selected. The system could deal with the side effects caused by impeller’s rotating speed up to 800RPM and ultrasound power of 400W. In term of power consumption, although mechanically stirred vessel consumes less energy (176W) than ultrasonicator (100–400W), ultrasonicator can potentially reduce the power consumption by changing the geometry or the direction of the probe.

Two-dimensional Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) (Dantec Dynamics, Denmark) and flow manager v40.00 (80S30) were used in order to measure the fluid behaviour under ultrasound irradiation. The PIV system was employed by using two double-shutter cameras with a resolution of 2048×2058pixel, dual-power 135–15 Laser, 2×135mJ at 15Hz, (pulse length of 2–9ns, and timing jitter of <0.5ns), a minimum time of 2–9ns between frames (81C92 Flow Sense EO 4M camera, 16Hz C-mount), and a high-performance 3D processor (precision T7500). The cameras were aligned to the laser sheet at a degree of
π / 4
. The liquid bulk was seeded with fluorescent particles of 50μm in diameter. These particles absorb the laser beam with a wavelength of 532nm and emit orange light (∼640nm). Water was used as a Newtonian fluid and the experiments were performed under atmospheric pressure and ambient temperature.

The PIV analysis was performed in a Cartesian co-ordinate to transform the image data into velocity vectors. Basically, two sequential images at the instant ‘t’ and ‘t +Δt’ describe the spatial positions of seeding particles in an array of integration area. The linear digital signal-processing model can describe this shift. PIV estimates the spatial shifting function s(m,n) based on the averaged values of f(m,n) and g(m,n) with an additional noise (at the scale of the interrogation area). The function f(m,n) and g(m,n) describe the light intensity within the interrogation area recorded at time t and t +Δt, respectively. The statistical technique of spatial cross-correction is the method used to estimate the displacement function s(m,n). Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is often used for cross-correlation calculations. Fourier transform is an image processing technique that decomposes an image into its sine and cosine components. The input image of this transformation represents a spatial domain while the output is a Fourier or frequency domain. The Fourier transform for a 2D Digital image with a dimension of M × N is defined as:
(1)
F ( u , v ) =
1
MN
∑
x = 0
M - 1
∑
y = 0
N - 1
f ( x , y )
e
- j 2 π ( ux / M + vy / N )
where x and y are the pixel coordinates in the image and f(x,y) is the image in the space. Inverse Fourier transform was then used again to re-transform the Fourier image into the spatial domain. The inverse Fourier transform is defined by:
(2)
f ( x , y ) =
∑
u = 0
M - 1
∑
ν = 0
N - 1
F ( u , v )
e
j 2 π ( ux / M + vy / N )

The final step of PIV processing is to identify the four highest correlation peaks and their positions in order to estimate the vector of particle motion. Therefore, Gaussian equation is often fitted to the highest correlation peak by using the adjacent correlation values to determine the location of the displacement peak from the pixel to sub-pixel.
The detected peak is the most probable destination of the particle displacement.

Knowing the time interval between the two pulses, velocity can be calculated as follows:
(3)
∫
t
t + Δ t
u (
t
′
)
dt
′
≈ Δ x
(4)
Δ x
Δ t
=
Δ X
M Δ t
≈ u
In which, Δx denotes the particle displacement during the illumination pulses of Δt and M is the magnification factor of the image system.

The model equations for the ultrasonicator containing liquid media and cavitation bubbles were derived based on the assumption that both phases could be described as continua. Therefore, the whole system was considered as the “Mixture model”, at which “no slip” condition was applied. Homogenous flow is assumed in a mixture model that is able to consider the evaporation/condensation of micro bubbles under ultrasound irradiation. In a mixture model, cavities are considered using the well-known Rayleigh–Plesset model that assumes plenty of nuclei for inception of microbubbles and considers their expansion and compression rates. In this study, CFD modelling was initiated by conducting mass and momentum balances through continuity and momentum balances. The general form of continuity equation is expressed by:
(5)
∂
∂ t
∑
k = 1
n
(

k
ρ
k
) + ∇ ·

k
) - ∇ · (
Γ
ε

α
k
) =
∑
k = 1
n
Γ
k

In this equation, k denotes the operating phase with the volume fraction of α, density of ρ and velocity of u at time of t and
∑
i = 1
2
α
i
= 1
. The mass transfer between the liquid and vapour due to cavitation in the ultrasonicator was considered through condensation (Rc ) and/or evaporation (Re ) rates. Therefore, the continuity equation for the mixture was changed to:
(6)
∂
ρ
m
∂ t

However, investigation of cavitation was not the objective of this work.

Stirred vessel modelling was performed in one continuous phase. Accordingly, the right side of the continuity equation was taken out and the continuity equation for this system then became:
(7)

(9)

where μ, g and F are the fluid viscosity, gravity acceleration and interfacial momentum exchange terms, respectively.

In most of the applications, transmitted acoustic energy is absorbed by liquid and converted into turbulent motion. High-speed impeller also provides strong turbulence in the stirred vessel. Accordingly, the standard k–ε model was employed for simulation of fluid flow in both the ultrasonicator and stirred vessel using Eq. (10) for turbulent kinetic energy and Eq. (11) for turbulent dissipation rate:
(10)
∂

where Gk and Gb represent the generation of turbulence kinetic energy due to mean velocity gradients and buoyancy. YM denotes the contribution of fluctuating dilatation in compressible turbulence to the overall dissipation rate.

are the turbulent constants. According to the recommendations of [9], the constants obtained after extensive examination of free turbulent flows, take the following values:
(12)

= 1.3

The above constants have been found appropriate for plane jets and mixing layers. These values yield satisfactory predictions for turbulent values, including the one near the walls. Sk and
S
ε
are user-defined source terms. Furthermore, Cai’s model [10] was employed to impose acoustic pressure on the media:
(13)

Here,

I
US
denote angular frequency, sound speed and intensity of ultrasound source. IUS refers to the ultrasound intensity.

Ultrasound waves are sinusoidal pressure waves that change with frequency. Therefore, the computational time step of CFD simulation should be smaller than the time period of the sound wave. An unsteady turbulent flow with time step of less than one-eighth the time of 24kHz ultrasound wave was considered in this study. The slip velocity was ignored. Isothermal condition was assumed for all experiments.

The fluid flow in the stirred vessel in this work was assumed transient, turbulent and adiabatic. Furthermore, cavitation that may occur in the highly turbulent regions with high energy absorption i.e. near impeller blades, was eliminated based on the assumption that the fluid had only one phase. It was also assumed that no vortex was generated as the liquid level was equal to the tank height and the reactor was capped and sealed. Various time steps were tested to obtain a stabilised solution. Moreover, the liquid velocity was initially considered zero. Similar to the sonoreactor, the initial gauge pressure and temperature in the stirred vessel were fixed at 0Pa and 300K, respectively.

Eqs. (1)–(13) were combined and solved numerically using the commercial CFD code (ANSYS/Fluent 13.5) in three dimensions. The fluid domain was generated and meshed using the same software. Different time steps, grid sizes and also grid types were applied in the simulation model and the results were checked in detail. The best values were then selected and employed for the simulation. In terms of time step for the ultrasonicator system, the values ranging from 1e−7 to 1e−5 were tested and their effects on the final results along with the optimum time needed to reach the stable conditions were considered. This range was chosen based on the time period of ultrasound frequency used (24kHz). The best results were obtained for the values ⩽5e−6. Accordingly, an unsteady turbulent flow with a time step of less than one-eighth the time period of 24kHz ultrasound wave was considered in this study, which was equal to 1e−6 s. The stirred vessel system was more sensitive to time step. Various time steps were tested to obtain a stabilised solution. The calculation started with a very small time step size (1e−7) and followed by increment to 1e−5 to speed up the calculation. Different grid sizes and types were investigated. Tetrahedral cells in both systems provided better results, especially under ultrasound irradiation. The results obtained by employing quadratic grids resulted in asymmetric and meaningless flow patterns. Considering the grid size, it was found that pressure distribution was significantly affected by the grid size in the vicinity of the ultrasound probe in the ultrasonicator and rotating domain in the stirred vessel. This is more critical in an ultrasonicator since negative and positive pressure pulses should be continuously spread within the system. Accordingly, different mesh sizes were investigated. Stable and similar results were obtained when finer grids were in the rotating region in the stirred vessel and vicinity of the probe in the ultrasonicator. Accordingly, a total of 499,758 tetrahedral cells were considered in the sonoreactor whereas 319,185 and 296,354 tetrahedral cells were employed for stationary and rotating domains of the stirred vessel, respectively. The initial values of fluid vapour (in sonoreactor) and velocity were set to zero in the whole domain. Pressure (as the sound pressure) was introduced into the system through Eq. (13) with four different power amplitudes of 100, 200, 300 and 400W. The other employed values in the equation included frequency of f =24kHz and sound speed of c =1480m/s in water. The transient stirrer motion was also modelled using the SM (Sliding Mesh) in which almost 72% of the vessel was considered stationary and the remaining (about 28%) was considered as the rotating region with rotating speeds of 400, 600 and 800RPM, respectively [11,12]. More details on boundary conditions of both systems are summarised in Table 3 and Fig. 1 .

SIMPLE algorithm was used to couple the continuity and Navier–Stokes equations in simulations of both ultrasound and stirred vessels, as this algorithm is the most stable and accurate. Furthermore, Under-Relaxation factors (URFs) are significant parameters controlling the stability and convergence of the iterative process and numerical scheme. Selection of suitable values for under-relaxation factors is a key to obtain an efficient numerical solution. In this study, the under relaxation factors were fixed as follows: pressure: 0.3, density: 0.5, vaporisation mass (in ultrasound case): 1, vapour (in ultrasound case): 0.2, body forces: 1.0, momentum: 0.7, turbulence: 0.3, turbulent kinetic energy: 0.8 and turbulent dissipation rate: 0.8.

Figs. 2 and 3
show the pressure profiles within the ultrasonicator and stirred vessel respectively. Based on Fig. 2, positive and negative pressure were generated and distributed within the ultrasonicator. Cavitation bubbles could be potentially generated in the areas with lower pressures near the transducer surface. Meanwhile, the distribution of dynamic pressure in the stirred vessel indicates the effect of impeller in its regions. In this study, lower pressure was observed above the impeller which experienced impeller suction. Greater values were found in regions near the bottom of the vessel and between the impeller and vessel walls. In the ultrasonicator, pressure depends on the acoustic pressure cycle governed by the transducer. Furthermore, the distribution of static pressure under ultrasound power was several times higher than that in the stirred vessel. The difference in the pressure distribution pattern under ultrasound irradiation and mechanical stirring is the other important point which can be clearly observed in the same figures.

Figs. 4–6
reveal the predicted flow pattern in the vertical and horizontal slices in both ultrasonicator and stirred vessel systems. As observed, only one fluid loop was generated under ultrasound irradiation that pushed the fluid downward and then upward. However, there were two circulation loops in the stirred vessel, one above the impeller and the other below it. In other words, a downward flow was generated in the center/below the impeller and an upward flow along the vessel walls. By following the path-lines illustrated in Fig. 5, it is observed that the two circulation loops appeared like a vertical infinity symbol, in which the upper loop had a smaller diameter than the lower one. It was attributed to higher suction flow rate above the impeller than the discharge flow rate below, which was also indicated by the total and statistical pressures presented in Fig. 3(a) and (b).

On the other hand, an axial flow was created in the sonoreactor in a downward direction from the transducer toward the top of the tank. Liquid flow pattern under ultrasound irradiation is severely affected by the direction and power of jet-like acoustic streaming. Therefore, different flow patterns are generated depends on these two factors. In other words, jet-like acoustic streaming acts as an input energy and motivating force. It induces the liquid to move and guides its motion within the system starting from the surface of the transducer. In this study, the probe was introduced from the top of the vessel into the liquid media which pushed the liquid to circulate from the top towards the bottom of the vessel. Accordingly, a uniform fluid circulation that covered all regions was generated by jet-like acoustic streaming in this study. This type of flow pattern is the most suitable for mixing of liquids with different densities as it prevents accumulation of denser liquid at the bottom or less dense liquid at the top (Fig. 4).

As observed in Fig. 5, the flow was discharged both axially and radially in the stirred vessel. It was found that the flow moved away from the blades at about 45˚ due to the angle of the blades. The flow was constrained in this system due to small radius of the vessel, resulting in incomplete liquid circulation and generation of two small circulation loops. Moreover, the flow pattern in both systems did not vary significantly when the power input (in sonoreactor) or impeller speed (in stirred vessel) increased. Therefore, both systems generated a stable flow pattern. This characteristic can considerably decrease the mixing time, especially in sonoreactors which generate only one circulation loop.

The magnitude of axial, radial and tangential velocities in the ultrasonicator and stirred vessel are compared in Figs. 7–11
. The effects of different power inputs of ultrasound irradiation and impeller speeds at different heights were also investigated.

The magnitude and profile of axial mean flow velocity in both the ultrasonicator and stirred vessel at different elevations and powers/impeller speeds are presented in Figs. 7 and 8, respectively. The highest axial velocity in the ultrasonicator was observed exactly below the transducer. The jet-like acoustic streaming with the maximum velocity of 145cm/s was generated in this region. However, the intensity of acoustic streaming decreased as it moved away from the transducer. It was because the other term of velocity (radial) became more prominent in such regions. According to Figs. 7 and 8, the axial velocity in the stirred vessel was basically low due to rotational flow in the vessel except for the impeller regions. In this domain, the axial flow pattern was generated with the highest value of 38.3cm/s between the suction (above the impeller) and discharge (below the impeller) regions.
The direction of axial velocities in the stirred vessel changed at a radius of around 2.2cm in the upper-half of the vessel and 1.3cm in the lower-half of the vessel.

Comparison between Fig. 7a and b showed that the flow interchange took place at the height of 5cm from the bottom of the stirred vessel from one circulation loop into another while a uniform circulation loop countered the flow pattern of the sonoreactor.

Fig. 8a and b presents the axial velocity profiles corresponding to the vessel elevations of 5 and 6cm from the bottom of the ultrasonicator and 3 and 7cm from the bottom of the stirred vessel at different power inputs and impeller rotating speeds, respectively. Generally, power input had more influence on the axial velocity of acoustic streaming under ultrasound irradiation. As observed, the axial velocity in this vessel increased from 43 to almost 145cm/s in the regions exactly under the transducer with the power amplitude increment from 100 to 400W. Meanwhile, increasing the impeller rotational speed in the stirred vessel from 400 to 800RPM resulted in axial velocity enhancement of only 20cm/s and 14.8cm/s in regions below and above the impeller.

Fig. 9a and b shows the distribution of radial velocities at different elevations of ultrasonicator and stirred vessel, respectively. The measured radial velocity at the perpendicular axis to the centre of the ultrasonicator (center axis of acoustic jet-like streaming) was almost zero since the flow pattern had only the axial factor in this region. The radial velocity under ultrasound was distinguished as the fluid changed toward the top of the ultrasonicator. Therefore, different radial velocities were observed at all elevations. This result was confirmed by the flow pattern induced by ultrasound energy as presented in Figs. 4 and 6 which showed that there was one uniform circulation in this type of reactor. However, the maximum value was observed at the bottom of the vessel where there was the largest circulation of fluid. The minimum radial velocity was observed in the regions near the transducer.

In contrast to the flow pattern in the ultrasonicator, the radial velocity in the stirred vessel decreased with increasing distance from the impeller. Besides, the radial velocity below the pitched blade impeller in the stirred vessel was slightly lower than that of above the impeller. However, Fig. 9b showed that there was greater radial velocity below the impeller. Therefore, the flow below the impeller was more axial than that above it.

Fig. 10(a) illustrates the effect of power enhancement on radial velocity in the ultrasonicator. As the power input increased, the radial velocity dramatically increased which indicated greater intensity of fluid circulation, resulting in decreased mixing time. Generally, the radial velocity increased by about 13cm/s with power enhancement. Accordingly, at the height of 6cm from the bottom of the vessel, the maximum radial velocity of 29cm/s was observed under the maximum ultrasound power input. The minimum velocity of 14.5cm/s was observed at the same height under minimum power amplitude.

Fig. 10b show that as the impeller speed increased from 400 to 800RPM, the maximum radial velocity increased from 3.96 to almost 19.3cm/s. Generally, radial velocity differed significantly with increasing impeller rotational speed in the stirred vessel. Besides, the values changed with the radius of the stirred vessel.
Fig. 11(a) and (b) presents the profiles of the tangential velocities in the stirred vessel, corresponding to the other two components presented in Figs. 7–11(b).

Near-zero tangenetial velocities in the ultrasonicator were observed in all locations. The tangential velocities were also nearly zero in the regions near the central axis of the stirred vessel. This was one of the major differences in the flow pattern between the ultrasonicator and stirred vessel.

The flow in the stirred vessel was highly rotational. Therefore, the highest tangential velocities (70.5cm/s) were observed in the vicinity of the impeller. As expected, the tangential velocities did not vary significantly with the height of the measurement in the stirred vessel except for those obtained at the top and bottom of the vessel which had the minimum value.
In addition, the tangential velocity measured in the impeller region significantly increased as the radius of the measured location increased along the radial direction.

Comparison between both vessels indicated that the axial and radial velocities in the ultrasonicator were generally higher than tangential component while tangential velocity countered the other velocity components in the stirred vessel.

After analysing the velocity compounds, the results can be summarised in one equation. Pandit et al. [13] suggested an excellent correlation based on ultrasound power intensity and distance from the ultrasound probe. It should be noted that the model obtained by Pandit et al. showed satisfying results in most regions of jet-like acoustic streaming. However, some overestimations were found in regions exactly under the ultrasound probe. It might be due to the validation range of the model (226–527kW/m2) used or some geometrical differences.

Based on the parameters selected by Pandit et al. [13], a new model was proposed to estimate the mean velocity of acoustic jet. The model is valid for the ultrasound irradiation intensity of 250–1000kW/m3, as below:
(14)
V
mean
= 1.14 ×
( I )
0.974
( Z )
- 0.164
( R - r )
R
0.702

In this equation, Vmean is the mean velocity of jet-like acoustic streaming (cm/s). Besides, I, Z, R and r also denote the intensity of irradiation (W/cm2), axial distance (cm), radius of reactor (cm) and radial distance from the center of the probe (cm), respectively. This equation predicts the mean velocity of jet flow with an accuracy of 98.1%. However, it is only valid for the jet-like acoustic streaming (
r ⩽
d
h
).

The same was performed for the stirred vessel as well:
(15)
V
mean
= 0.579 ×
( N )
2.12
( Z )
- 0.41
r
R
0.65
Here, V mean represents the mean velocity within the vessel (cm/s) and N implies the impeller rotating speed (R/s). Z, R and r denote the same meaning as explained above for Eq. (14). This equation is valid for a stirred vessel equipped with pitched blades with a rotating speed ranging from 400 to 800RPM or 6.67 to 13.33RPS. Eq. (15) can be used to obtain the mean velocity within the stirred vessel with an accuracy of 94.8%.

In this study, PIV was used for both analysis and validation of results obtained by the CFD simulation. Based on Fig. 12 , the acoustic streaming obtained by PIV analysis at four different power amplitudes (100, 200, 300, 400W) is presented and compared with that from CFD simulation. These graphs give an immediate rendering of the spatial flow pattern induced by power ultrasound irradiation. As observed, the jet-like acoustic streaming and recirculation flow were revealed by the vectors perpendicular to the surface of the transducer and directed diagonally against the transducer near the reactor walls. According to the PIV results, the velocity increased with power amplitude until the maximum jet-flow velocity of about 135–148cm/s in the system although the field was not fully symmetric. Besides, the velocity at the highest power was almost 5 times greater than that of the lowest power. Fig. 12 also showed that the axial velocity decreased with increasing distance from the probe in the radial direction. However, it did not significantly change in the axial direction.

The results corresponded to the velocity obtained from the center of the jet-like acoustic streaming from both the CFD and PIV techniques for 4 different sonication powers are also summarised in Fig. 13 . The results showed a reasonable agreement. In term of types of flow pattern, the flow pattern established by the CFD simulation was similar to that obtained by PIV. However, PIV analysis of mechanical stirring was not conducted in this study, attributed to air entrainment into liquid at impeller rotational speeds above 300RPM. The bubbles of air break and spread within the system will disturb the image captured by PIV system.

The distribution of the turbulence kinetic energy in both the ultrasonicator and stirred vessel are presented in Fig. 14 a and b. The turbulence intensity is defined as the ratio of the root mean square of the turbulent velocity fluctuations (
u
′
) to the mean velocity (U):
(16)
I ≡
u
′
U

At which,
u
′
=
1 / 3
u
x
′ 2
+
u
y
′ 2
+
u
z
′ 2
and
U =
1 / 3
U
x
2
+
U
y
2
+
U
z
2
.

As expected from the fluid flow patterns and pressure profiles, the turbulence kinetic energy was at the maximum level in the vicinity of the impeller in the stirred vessel and below the transducer due to the presence of acoustic streaming in the utrasonicator. The lowest turbulence kinetic energy was found in the region near the bottom and the free surface in both systems. However, the lowest kinetic energy of 18.7% in the stirred vessel was prominently lower than that in the ultrasonicator (66%). Generally, it can be suggested that there was higher turbulence in the ultrasonicator than the stirred vessels.

On average, higher turbulence kinetic energy was observed above the impeller in the stirred vessel compared to below the impeller. Moreover, the value decreased in regions away from the impeller. In the ultrasonicator, the kinetic energy decreased as the acoustic streaming moved closer to the bottom of the reactor.

It was observed that turbulent kinetic energies in both systems were critically affected by the power input. Therefore, the kinetic energy increased from 96(%) to 138(%) as the power amplitude increased from 100 to 400W. Likewise, the kinetic energy increased from 28% to 82% with increasing impeller speed from 400 to 800RPM (Fig. 14b). Due to weak velocity in some shear zones, the turbulence intensity parameter induced values much higher than 100% in regions near the transducer or in vicinity of the impeller.

Aside from the turbulent kinetic energy, the type of flow under ultrasound energy can be determined by Reynolds number (R), where
R = ( ρ VD ) / μ
. Here, D and V denote the local jet-diameter and its centerline velocity, respectively. Reynolds number of 3000 is the critical value for onset of turbulence [14]. Reynolds number in this study reflected the condition of turbulent flow over almost the whole jet length under the sonication power of 400, 300 and 200watt with a corresponding Re number starting from 35.8e3, 25.95e3 and 17.79e3 to 7.4e3, 5.68e3 and 4.45e3, respectively. However, the jet-like acoustic streaming fell within the range of Reynolds numbers at its middle length, displaying transitional behaviour. This means that the jet behaved like a turbulent flow (Re ∼10.628e3) initially and then fell within the laminar range (Re ∼2.471e3).

There are a few methods for analysing the mixing time in sonoreactors which are measurement of dissolved oxygen, conductivity, pH, Particle Image Velocimetry and theoretical investigations using computational fluid dynamics (CFD). In general, although injection of tracers (such as salt that change the media conductivity) and detecting or monitoring the trace are the most widely used methods, introduction of a device to detect the trace can add a substantial volume to the system which distorts the RTD.

Analysing and estimating mixing time using CFD simulation have recently been widely used. As an example, Kumar et al. [15] investigated the dependency of mixing time on the ratio of horn diameter to that of the reactor (D/T) experimentally using tracer conductivity and numerical study through CFD simulation. They found that the mean velocity increased with a decrease in D/T ratio and the sonoreactor with smaller D/T ratio presented lower mixing time. It was attributed to the average turbulent kinetic energy which was higher for the smaller vessel. Considering the effect of intensity of ultrasound irradiation, Kumar et al. also stated that the mixing time reduced from 131 to 17s by increasing ultrasound power from 15 to 35kW/m3.

Rahimi et al. also employed CFD simulation to analyse the mixing phenomena in a sonoreactor with a diameter of 14cm and height of 15cm, equipped with four high-frequency piezoelectric transducers (1.7MHz) with a diameter of 2cm.

Some other researchers have also suggested different correlations based on the experimental results obtained by the tracer response. As an instance, Vichare et al. correlated the mixing time in a low frequency/low power sonoreactor (22.7kHz, 24.5W) with geometrical parameters to reach sufficient mixing. The model, considers the dimensions of the reactor, horn tip and location of the transducer:
(17)
θ
mix
= 7 ×
10
6
d
- 0.235
Z
3 / 2
T
3
( T + 2 Z )
- 2
d
h
- 4
ν
h
2
g
1 / 2
μ
- 2
ρ
l
2

In this equation,
θ
mix
is mixing time (s) and d, Z, T, dh , vh, ρl denote the distance between horn tip and bottom of the reactor (m), height of liquid in reactor (m), reactor diameter (m), tip diameter of horn (m), velocity of horn (m/s), viscosity of liquid (N s/m2) and bulk density of liquid (kg/m3), respectively. Similar model for the system of blended homogenous fuel was also suggested by [16]. However, the correlations suggested are mostly applicable for a special range of parameters or the vessels with suitable designs (on the basis of correlated one).

As stated by most researches, mixing time estimated by CFD was found to be comparable with that estimated using the experimental techniques. Based on the previous studies that investigated the effect of ultrasound wave frequencies ranging from 20 to 955kHz on mixing time, lower frequencies were found to generate more uniform mixing due to stronger contribution of the physical effects of cavitation phenomena. Reduction of mixing time by increasing intensity of irradiation has also been confirmed by other researches.

In order to characterise and compare the mixing performance in the vessel, the axial mixing time was analysed. Therefore, the axial flow rate at different elevations in both vessels was obtained from the CFD results. Accordingly, the axial velocity (either
υ
z
+
or)
υ
z
+
components were integrated at particular horizontal planes through:
(18)
Q
az
( z ) =
∫
A
υ
z
-
dA =
∫
A
υ
z
+
dA

In this equation, A is the radial section of the vessel. The average axial flow rate for the entire vessel was then calculated by the following equation:
(19)
Q
Aax
( z ) =
∫
0
H
Q
ax
( z ) dz
∫
0
H
dz

Therefore, the average axial circulation time was obtained as follows:
(20)
t
ax
=
V
Q
Aax

Based on the above calculations, the minimum circulation time of 1.38 and 3.18s were obtained under ultrasound power of 400W and rotating speed of 800RPM in the stirred vessel. The values increased to almost 4.86s as the ultrasound power decreased to almost 100W. Meanwhile, the circulation time in the stirred vessel increased to 4.21 and 5.62s at the rotating speed of 600 and 400RPM. The axial circulation time clearly indicated that there were higher axial flow rate and overall mixing efficiency under ultrasound irradiation compared to stirred vessel.

3D-CFD simulation was used to investigate and characterise the jet-like acoustic streaming under ultrasound irradiation. Ultrasonication was performed by a low-frequency (24kHz) and high-power (100–400W) transducer. The acoustic pressure was modelled based on Cai et al. [10]’s formulation. The three dimensional model was implemented in Eulerian coordinate. Accordingly, the pressure distribution, velocity (axial, radial, tangential components), acoustic jet-like streaming, fluid flow pattern, axial mixing time and turbulence were investigated. The results were also compared with that obtained in a classic mechanically stirred vessel. Mechanical stirring was conducted by a six-pitched-blade impeller with 45°-blades at a rotating speed of 400–800RPM. The CFD results indicated that the maximum velocity induced by acoustic jet-like streaming can reach the value of 145cm/s. Furthermore, the axial velocity component was prominent under ultrasound irradiation while the tangential component was more significant in the stirred vessel. Accordingly, the axial mixing time under ultrasound irradiation was about 1.2–2.3 times shorter than that of mechanically stirred vessel. Besides, the maximum kinetic energy of 138% in ultrasonicator was prominently higher than that in the stirred vessel generally. Finally, Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) was employed to analyse the acoustic jet streaming, axial velocity and flow pattern generated under ultrasound irradiation. The results were in great agreement with that predicted by the CFD simulation.
